course_title: Performative Communication: Presentation and Public Speaking

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate foundational skills in vocal performance including postural alignment, breath support, resonance, and clear articulation.
Plan, structure and deliver a presentation for a specific audience with professional integrity.
Utilise rhetorical and expressive performative devices to enhance the impact of a presentation.
Critically analyse, apply and reflect on the methods and theories of performative communication.

assessments.task_description:
From Weeks 2 – 11, your tutorial participation will be assessed through written and practical tasks.  

Part 1: Written Activities (20%) Based on set readings and tutorial activities. Completed during tutorials, in 20 mins.

In Weeks 2, 4, 6, 8 and 11 you will complete and submit a 100-word written reflection during class time that connects tutorial activities with a key point from one of the set readings.  There is no written task in Week 10 due to the public holiday.  Each submission is worth 5 marks. The best four submissions will make up your final mark.   

There are 5 opportunities across the semester, each worth 5%. Only your best 4 results count, together forming 20% of your final grade. Because only 4 of the 5 count, the assessment already builds in the flexibility to miss one session — for illness or any other reason — with no penalty and no documentation required.

There are no opportunities to re-take an individual exercise, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below. As this is an internal course, you must make yourself available to complete each exercise at the scheduled date, time and venue. These are handwritten, closed-book tasks: no written material, electronic material or electronic devices may be used. All such items must remain in your bag or under your desk, and all electronic devices must be switched off or in airplane mode. If a device emits any noise, you may be subject to an allegation of misconduct and the item may be confiscated for the duration of the exercise. You may bring suitable writing implements (in a clear container), water in a clear bottle with the label removed, and disposable earplugs.


Part 2:  Practical Activities: (20%). Completed during tutorials. Each presentation is 1- 2 minutes in length.

In Weeks 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 you will present a short presentation during class time to demonstrate your developing skills in Performative Communication.  Details for each presentation will be provided in Lectures and course materials. Each submission is worth 5 marks. The best four presentations will make up your final mark.  

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. There are 5 opportunities across the semester, each worth 5%. Only your best 4 results count, together forming 20% of your final grade. Because only 4 of the 5 count, the assessment already builds in the flexibility to miss one session — for illness or any other reason — with no penalty and no documentation required.

There are no opportunities to re-take an individual exercise, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below. As this is an internal course, you must make yourself available to complete each exercise at the scheduled date, time and venue.


Alternative assessment and accommodations

If you have a Disability Action Plan relevant to this task, please contact the course coordinator as early as possible. Reasonable accommodations can be made for matters documented in a Disability Action Plan (for example, if you are unable to handwrite under timed conditions).

The 1-session buffer for both Part 1 and Part 2 (2 sessions in total) described above is intended to cover ordinary absences. A separate make-up session is available only to students who, for a genuine and documented reason, miss more than 1 of the exercises — for example, a sudden or serious illness, or another documented cause requiring a lengthy absence. Missing 1 session for Part 1, and 1 session for Part 2 is already covered by the assessment structure and does not qualify for a make-up.

To be considered for a make-up session or any other alternative arrangement, you must contact the course coordinator with your documentation as soon as possible after the circumstances arise. One make-up session will be offered in the Week 13 lecture slot; if you are eligible, please ensure you are available.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
In Week 12, you will deliver a 2-minute persuasive pitch for a project, product, or concept. Your presentation must be supported by a concise 1–3 slide visual deck. 

This task evaluates your ability to: 

  • Strategize: Design and deliver a pitch tailored to a specific audience and objective. 
  • Engage grounded physicality and breath support to maximise vocal presence  
  • Use vocal variety and resonance to engage an audience  
  • Utilise crisp and clear articulation to express your message  
  • Argue Effectively: Construct a compelling, logical case for your chosen concept. 
  • Influence: Integrate rhetorical strategies and expressive devices to maximize the impact of your delivery. 


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment

  • All Pitches will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of:
  • Moderation of marking;
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.

assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
In this Reflective Essay, you will analyse your progress through the course in relation to key theories and practices of performative communication.  

This task will assess your ability to:  

  • Analyse the strengths and weaknesses of your pitch presentation  
  • Apply information from course readings to personal skill development  
  • Identify achievements made through the course and areas for future development.  


The referencing style used for Drama assignments is MLA, 9th edition. 

A detailed assignment brief will be made available via Blackboard in due course. 

Criteria:  

  • Treatment of Topic (depth and insight) and Integration of Research  
  • Rigour of Argumentation  
  • Organisation and structure  
  • Expression and style  
  • MLA Referencing Conventions
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Introduction and Presence.

LECTURE: The importance of strong presentation skills across all professions and pursuits; In-person presentation in the digital age. Course logistics - including what to wear and bring to tutorials.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1:  Introduction and Presence. LECTURE: The importance of strong presentation skills across all professions and pursuits; In-person presentation in the digital age. Course logistics - including what to wear and bring to tutorials.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: No tutorials

No tutorials in week 1.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: No tutorials No tutorials in week 1.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: The Body and The Voice.

LECTURE: The art of verbal persuasion; Constructing an argument; Logos, Pathos, and Ethos; Language as Action. READING: - The three modes of persuasion (53pp) from Classical Rhetoric for the Modern Student by Edwards Corbett & Robert Connors.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: The Body and The Voice. LECTURE: The art of verbal persuasion; Constructing an argument; Logos, Pathos, and Ethos; Language as Action. READING: - The three modes of persuasion (53pp) from Classical Rhetoric for the Modern Student by Edwards Corbett & Robert Connors.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: The Body and The Voice.

TUTORIAL: Dissecting dramatic speeches and short scenes in rhetorical terms. Construction of argument and modes of persuasion in action.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: The Body and The Voice. TUTORIAL: Dissecting dramatic speeches and short scenes in rhetorical terms. Construction of argument and modes of persuasion in action.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Freeing the Voice.

LECTURE: What is presence?; The importance of proprioception and physical awareness in presentation/performance. READINGS: - Alexander: the man and his discovery (17pp) from Body Learning by Michael Gelb. - The Self-Image (15pp) from Awareness Through Movement by Moshe Feldenkrais. - The Attainment of Poise (16pp) by Raymond Dart (1947), South African Medical Journal.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3:  Freeing the Voice. LECTURE: What is presence?; The importance of proprioception and physical awareness in presentation/performance. READINGS: - Alexander: the man and his discovery (17pp) from Body Learning by Michael Gelb. - The Self-Image (15pp) from Awareness Through Movement by Moshe Feldenkrais. - The Attainment of Poise (16pp) by Raymond Dart (1947), South African Medical Journal .

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Freeing the Voice.

TUTORIAL: Alexander, Feldenkrais, and Pure Movement work
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Freeing the Voice. TUTORIAL: Alexander, Feldenkrais, and Pure Movement work

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Improvisation, Play and Presence

LECTURE: Body language, gesture, and movement; What we read when we see a body in space. READINGS: - Boal, Blau, Brecht: the body by Philip Auslander (19pp), in Playing Boal, ed. Mady Schutzman & Jan Cohen-Cruz (Routledge, 1994). - Low-Achieving Readers, High Expectations: Image Theatre Encourages Critical Literacy by Carol Rozansky & Colleen Aagesen (18pp), in Journal of Adolescent & Adult Literacy 53(6).
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4:  Improvisation, Play and Presence LECTURE: Body language, gesture, and movement; What we read when we see a body in space. READINGS: - Boal, Blau, Brecht: the body by Philip Auslander (19pp), in Playing Boal , ed. Mady Schutzman & Jan Cohen-Cruz (Routledge, 1994). - Low-Achieving Readers, High Expectations: Image Theatre Encourages Critical Literacy by Carol Rozansky & Colleen Aagesen (18pp), in Journal of Adolescent & Adult Literacy 53(6).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Improvisation, Play and Presence

TUTORIAL: Develop both reading and creating of ideas and intentions through the body using Image Theatre.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4:  Improvisation, Play and Presence TUTORIAL: Develop both reading and creating of ideas and intentions through the body using Image Theatre.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Rhetoric and Persuasion

LECTURE: What the voice is for; How the voice works; Why the voice sometimes doesn't work; Effective vocal expression and communication of thoughts and feelings. READINGS: - Articles by Linklater, Lessac, Berry and Rodenburg (40pp) from The Vocal Vision (ed. Marion Hampton and Barbara Acker). - Introduction and Declaring Your Vocal Rights (15pp) from The Right to Speak by Patsy Rodenburg.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Rhetoric and Persuasion LECTURE: What the voice is for; How the voice works; Why the voice sometimes doesn't work; Effective vocal expression and communication of thoughts and feelings. READINGS: - Articles by Linklater, Lessac, Berry and Rodenburg (40pp) from The Vocal Vision (ed. Marion Hampton and Barbara Acker). - Introduction and Declaring Your Vocal Rights (15pp) from The Right to Speak by Patsy Rodenburg.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Rhetoric and Persuasion

TUTORIAL: Connection between impulse, body, breath, and sound; Exercises drawn from the the early part of the Linklater Progression.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Rhetoric and Persuasion TUTORIAL: Connection between impulse, body, breath, and sound; Exercises drawn from the the early part of the Linklater Progression.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Developing your voice

LECTURE: Communication, not projection ; Vocal presence and command; What makes us want to listen to a voice?; Why are some voices engaging and inviting while others are off-putting or annoying? READINGS: - An Introduction: the approach to vocal freedom (22pp) from Freeing the Natural Voice by Kristin Linklater. - The Moment of Nothing: a philosophical note on the work of Kristin Linklater (19pp) by David Farrell Krell, Mosaic 43/4. Continue working on haiku or short poem.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: Developing your voice LECTURE: Communication, not projection ; Vocal presence and command; What makes us want to listen to a voice?; Why are some voices engaging and inviting while others are off-putting or annoying? READINGS: - An Introduction: the approach to vocal freedom (22pp) from Freeing the Natural Voice by Kristin Linklater. - The Moment of Nothing: a philosophical note on the work of Kristin Linklater (19pp) by David Farrell Krell, Mosaic 43/4. Continue working on haiku or short poem.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Developing your voice

TUTORIAL: Increasing vibrations without force or projection; Release of habitual tensions which inhibit the voice; Exercises from the middle (freeing) part of the Linklater progression.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: Developing your voice TUTORIAL: Increasing vibrations without force or projection; Release of habitual tensions which inhibit the voice; Exercises from the middle (freeing) part of the Linklater progression.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Affecting your audience

LECTURE: Resonance and the different personalities in every voice; Articulation as clarity of thought and intention; Why not 'enunciation'? READING: - Introduction from Voice in Modern Theatre by Jacqueline Martin (Routledge 1991). TBA. Continue working and then present haiku or short poem (ASSESSED)
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Affecting your audience LECTURE: Resonance and the different personalities in every voice; Articulation as clarity of thought and intention; Why not 'enunciation'? READING: - Introduction from Voice in Modern Theatre by Jacqueline Martin (Routledge 1991). TBA. Continue working and then present haiku or short poem (ASSESSED)

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Present Draft 1 of Pitch Presentation for feedback

TUTORIAL: Exploring the resonators and working on range; Articulation; Exercises from the latter part (resonators, range and articulation) of the Linklater progression. (ASSESSED)
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 7: Present Draft 1 of Pitch Presentation for feedback
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Present Draft 1 of Pitch Presentation for feedback TUTORIAL: Exploring the resonators and working on range; Articulation; Exercises from the latter part (resonators, range and articulation) of the Linklater progression. (ASSESSED)

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Voice and Personal Expression

LECTURE: Scenic improvisation; Applied improvisation in a variety of contexts. READING: - Whose Classroom is it Anyway: improvisation as a teaching tool by RA Berk and RH Trieber (31pp), in Journal on Excellence in College Teaching 20(3) (2009). Improvised scenes (ASSESSED)
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: Voice and Personal Expression LECTURE: Scenic improvisation; Applied improvisation in a variety of contexts. READING: - Whose Classroom is it Anyway: improvisation as a teaching tool by RA Berk and RH Trieber (31pp), in Journal on Excellence in College Teaching 20(3) (2009). Improvised scenes (ASSESSED)

Week 8: Voice and Personal Expression

TUTORIAL: Long-form improvisation drills. Improvised scenes (ASSESSED)
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: Voice and Personal Expression TUTORIAL: Long-form improvisation drills. Improvised scenes (ASSESSED)

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Developing your Pitch

LECTURE: Basic (linguistic) register theory, studies on register; Examples of register choice for different presentation/performance situations; Deliberate manipulation of register in presentation. READING: - Theatre and Meta-theatre in Hamlet by Kate Flaherty (15pp), in Sydney Studies in English 31 (2005). Students start working on their final presentations.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9:  Developing your Pitch LECTURE: Basic (linguistic) register theory, studies on register; Examples of register choice for different presentation/performance situations; Deliberate manipulation of register in presentation. READING: - Theatre and Meta-theatre in Hamlet by Kate Flaherty (15pp), in Sydney Studies in English 31 (2005). Students start working on their final presentations.

TUTORIAL: Explore register differences between different performative activities.
